title: mu-ovl-A: Enrich SpanKind to projection-relevant variants
description:
Sub-bead of mu-ovl (experiment 4 / 2026-05-13d). Wire-surface phase per mu repo convention.

## Scope

IN: extend `SpanKind` in `crates/mu-core/src/context/rope.rs:71-81` with the variants needed for projection differentiation:
- `ToolCall` — outgoing tool invocation from the assistant
- `ToolSchema` — registered tool definition consumed at session start
- `SkillActivation` — span emitted when a skill becomes active
- `MemoryInjection` — content pulled in via memory recall
- `Compaction` — synthetic span replacing N evicted spans
- `FileLoad` — content loaded from a file (e.g., spec/skill/source file)

Keep the four existing variants (`System`, `User`, `Assistant`, `ToolResult`).

Update `From<SpanKind> for ProviderRole` in `renderer.rs:74` to map the new kinds — most map to `System` (schema, skill activation, memory injection, compaction, file load) or `ToolResult` (tool call result, actual ToolResult). Document the mapping rationale inline.

OUT: rendering rule changes (that's mu-ovl-B). The renderer still produces the existing shallow OperatorView differentiation; this phase only adds the discriminator.

## Acceptance

- New `SpanKind` variants land with serde rename_all snake_case
- `From<SpanKind> for ProviderRole` exhaustive (compiler-enforced)
- Existing 4 retention-class + rope tests still green
- 1-2 new tests: serde round-trip for new variants; ProviderRole mapping coverage
- `cargo test --workspace` green at commit

## References

- Existing SpanKind: `crates/mu-core/src/context/rope.rs:70-81`
- Existing role mapping: `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:74-82`
- Spec: `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (section 'Skills, tools, and the active context as a retained pointer set', lines 538+)
- Experiment doc: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-2026-05-13d-mu-ovl.md`

title: mu-036 Phase B: RunMode + handle_start_autonomous + SelfReport loop integration
description:
## Why

Phase A (sister-bead, lands first overnight) adds the wire types, capability extension, and event payloads for mu-036 with no behavioral changes — the dispatch handlers return 'not-yet-implemented' errors. This bead is the next phase: actually wire the autonomous loop.

## What

* Add `RunMode` enum to mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs: Idle | Asking | Autonomous { iteration, goal, options, started_at, tool_calls_consumed } | Sleeping.
* handle_start_autonomous in dispatch.rs: validates session capability includes AutonomyCapability::Allowed, enqueues an AgentInput::StartAutonomous into the session's input channel, returns accepted.
* Agent loop's outer driver: when in Autonomous, the iteration cycle is decide → act → goal-check → decide-next instead of waiting on user inputs. SelfReport goal check first (delegate-grader is Phase D).
* Emit AutonomousIterationStarted / Completed events around each iteration (event payloads land in Phase A).
* Bound enforcement at every iteration boundary: max_iterations, max_wall_clock_ms, max_total_tool_calls_in_autonomy (from the session's Capability, NOT the request options — INV-2).
* Tests: scripted Autonomous over FauxProvider; iteration count exactly hits the bound; SelfReport goal=satisfied terminates early.

## Out of scope (separate beads)

* Phase C: schedule_wakeup + Sleeping state
* Phase D: DelegateGrader + ExternalSignal goal check methods
* Escalate-on-idle (INV-4 mention)
* TUI surface for autonomous sessions

## Acceptance

Workspace tests green; new tests cover the iteration cycle + bound enforcement against FauxProvider; AutonomousTerminated is always the final event (INV-7).

title: Future: secret-aware prompt/content scanning and redaction
description:
Add pre-send/pre-log secret scanning and optional content decoupling. Baseline should be default-on, configurable mechanical local scanners (rg/regex/entropy/prefix) for API keys, PEM blocks, JWTs, provider tokens, .env-style assignments, high-entropy strings, etc.

Events may store content_sha256/content_ref/redaction_status/redacted_preview rather than full text when appropriate; plaintext can live in a local/encrypted/optional content store.

Optional later layer: fast local-only LLM/classifier for semantic sensitivity (HIPAA, classified markings, corporate IP), advisory/risk-scoring only — not the sole guard. Remote models must never be used to detect secrets before sending, because that is already exfiltration.

Related memories: bc1d46ec, ac66f02f.

title: session.set_provider RPC: mid-session model swap
description:
## Why

Companion to mu-j1b (per-ask provider override). That bead is for one-shot overrides; this one is for the *persistent* swap: 'from now on, this session uses sonnet'. Use case: a session that started cheap (haiku) and now needs more capability — current workaround is close+create a fresh session, losing the in-memory context (assistant messages, tool result history).

## What

New RPC: `session.set_provider`. Wire:

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "provider": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929" }
}
```

Response: `{ "swapped": true, "from": {...}, "to": {...} }` or an error if validation fails.

Mutates the session's stored ProviderSelector. Subsequent asks (with no override) use the new provider.

## Considerations

- **Conversation context portability**: the existing AgentMessage history is provider-agnostic (it's mu's internal types, not provider-specific format). So swapping should JUST WORK from a state perspective — the next translate_messages call will render the same history into the new provider's wire shape.
- **Tool compatibility**: the new provider must support the session's currently-bound tools. Validate at swap time and fail with an explanatory error rather than at next ask time.
- **Cache invalidation**: like mu-j1b, the new provider has no cache hit on the old prefix. Document this in the response so callers can decide whether to swap mid-session.
- **Should this emit a SessionCreated-style event?** It's a state change worth a durable record. Probably yes — a new EventPayload::ProviderSwapped { from, to, at_event_id } variant. mu-pex's aggregator would need to be updated to handle the case where a session's (provider, model) changes mid-stream: maybe split the session into two virtual sessions in usage_history rows. Phase-2 detail.

## Acceptance

- Swap succeeds; subsequent asks use the new provider.
- Session's event log records the swap.
- Validation error when the new provider can't serve the session's tools.

## Cross-references

- mu-j1b — per-ask provider override (this bead's complement)
- mu-pex Phase 2 — aggregator behavior under mid-session provider swap

title: kqueue/inotify watches on file-loaded spans, with signal/policy separation
description:
When a span is loaded from a file (skill, tool schema, system prompt template, CLAUDE.md), register an OS file watch. On change, emit a structured event. Policy on the event is separate from the signal.

## Mechanism

FreeBSD: kqueue (EVFILT_VNODE, NOTE_WRITE|DELETE|RENAME). Linux: inotify (IN_MODIFY|DELETE|MOVE). macOS: kqueue or FSEvents. Cross-platform via notify crate or cfg-gated.

Emits SourceChangedEvent { span_id, source_path, change_kind, detected_at }.

## Policies (separate from signal)

Default: don't auto-reload. Rope addresses version-at-activation-time. Preserves reproducibility — 'what did the model know at turn N?' answerable consistently before and after a file change.

Explicit: rope.rehydrate(span_id) reloads and emits span.rehydrated event.

Configurable (deferred follow-up): auto_rehydrate_on_change false/true; auto_rehydrate_for_kinds list.

## Scope

In: notify crate (verify FreeBSD support before commit); SourceChangedEvent emission; RetainedRope::rehydrate API; tests (temp dir + touch); doc section in event-sourced-context.md.

Out: per-session policy config (follow-up bead); TUI stale-span indicator (operator-view enhancement); directory watches for skill-with-references (needs design pass).

## Why

Reproducibility by default; reload is explicit and audited; multiple policies coexist (dev auto-rehydrates skills; prod never); watch is cheap regardless of policy.

## Acceptance

File watches register on span load; modifying a watched file emits SourceChangedEvent within <1s latency; default policy: span unchanged after event (only rehydrate updates); rehydrate emits separate event; tested at minimum on FreeBSD (kqueue); workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-6-file-watch-source-changes.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-nat
Memory: 3cc7a18c

title: TUI: system_prompt palette commands + Ctrl-X Ctrl-P $EDITOR chord
description:
## Why

mu-n48 (commit xollnzmv 2026-05-11/12) threads system_prompt through Provider::stream and surfaces it in the wire protocol. But the TUI doesn't expose it: pressing 'n' always sends `CreateSessionRequest { provider, system_prompt: None }`. Closing this gap completes mu-n48's user-visible story.

## What

Two complementary affordances:

### 1. Palette commands

```
:system_prompt                  → show current default in firehose
:system_prompt <one-line text>  → set as default for next 'n'
:clear_system_prompt            → unset
```

Stored as `App.default_system_prompt: Option<String>` alongside the existing `default_provider` tuple.

### 2. Ctrl-X Ctrl-P chord ('P' for Prompt)

In SendPrompt mode (after pressing 'i'), pressing Ctrl-X then Ctrl-P bounces the **current default system_prompt** into $EDITOR — same handoff machinery as mu-82l's Ctrl-X Ctrl-E for the user prompt. Empty buffer if no current default; on editor save, store as the new default. Trailing \n stripped.

Most system prompts are multi-paragraph and benefit from a real editor; one-line palette form is for quick toggles.

### 3. Header display

When a default is set, header shows `sys: ✓` (with content as a tooltip via :system_prompt to display, not inline — system prompts are too long to dump inline).

## Wiring

`create_session` JSON now includes system_prompt when set:

```json
{
  "provider": { "kind": "...", "model": "..." },
  "system_prompt": "..."
}
```

The wire field has been accepted since mu-n48; this just populates it from the TUI.

## Acceptance

- :system_prompt 'be concise' sets the prompt; next 'n' creates a session that respects it.
- Ctrl-X Ctrl-P opens $EDITOR with current default, save updates it, :cq leaves unchanged.
- :clear_system_prompt removes the default; next 'n' sends `system_prompt: None`.
- F5 cache% column rises on multi-ask sessions when a meaningful system prompt is set (the live-verify already showed +~2.5% cache hit rate for an 80-token prompt).

## Cross-references

- mu-n48: provider wiring
- mu-82l: $EDITOR chord pattern (reuse `open_in_editor`)
- mu-i6j: cache_control on tools; the system prompt joins it as cached prefix

title: TUI: $EDITOR integration for prompt editing (Ctrl-X Ctrl-E)
description:
## Why

For prompts longer than a few lines, in-place editing in the TUI is limiting even after mu-wd2 (basic readline). zsh's edit-command-line binding (`Ctrl-X Ctrl-E`) is the canonical pattern for 'this is too long to type comfortably here; let me bounce into a real editor'.

Discussed 2026-05-11. Helix-core embedding was considered and rejected — $EDITOR delivers the same value (you get any editor, including Helix when $EDITOR=hx) at a fraction of the implementation cost.

## How the pattern works

The TUI's state lives in-memory (App struct in Rust). The terminal display is the *output* of that state, not the storage. So the editor handoff is just 'who currently owns the terminal':

1. Persist current prompt buffer to a tempfile (with a .md suffix so editors syntax-highlight markdown by default).
2. Tear down our terminal grip: `disable_raw_mode()`, `execute!(stdout(), LeaveAlternateScreen)`. The shell's prior scrollback comes back.
3. Spawn $EDITOR (default 'vi' if unset) on the tempfile. It inherits stdin/stdout/stderr from us; it owns the terminal.
4. Block on the editor's status (synchronous; UX-wise, the TUI 'pauses' while the editor is open).
5. On editor exit: `execute!(stdout(), EnterAlternateScreen)`, `enable_raw_mode()`. We're back in a fresh alt-screen canvas; next render tick repaints the App.
6. Read the tempfile back, populate the prompt buffer.
7. Clean up the tempfile.

Total: ~30 LOC plus a key-binding entry.

## Keybinding

Ctrl-X Ctrl-E (matching zsh / bash readline). Or, if Ctrl-X is reserved for something else in the TUI, F12, or :edit palette command.

## Edge cases

- Editor exits with non-zero status (user did :cq in vi to abort): treat as cancel — don't update the prompt buffer.
- Tempfile cleanup on panic: use a RAII guard (`tempfile::NamedTempFile` does this).
- Slow editors / large files: not a real concern for prompts; mu's prompts are typically <10KB. If this ever matters, async/spawn-thread variants exist.

## Acceptance

- Pressing Ctrl-X Ctrl-E from input mode opens $EDITOR with current prompt content.
- Saving + exiting populates the prompt buffer with the edited content.
- Cancelling (non-zero exit) leaves the prompt buffer unchanged.
- Works with vi, nvim, hx, nano, ed in CI tests (or at minimum, a faux-editor that just echoes 'X' into the file).

## Cross-reference

- mu-wd2 (arrow keys in-place) — complement, not replacement. Most short prompts will still be edited in-place; $EDITOR is for the long-prompt case.

title: Session templates: startup-time spawn presets for common (provider, model) combos
description:
## Why

Filed 2026-05-12 from the design discussion after mu-ium landed.

User workflow surfacing: typical daily work involves a small recurring set of (provider, model) sessions — e.g. opus-4-7 for hard reasoning, haiku-4-5 for cheap subtasks, gpt-5.5 for codex routing. Today, opening these requires 3+ rounds of `:provider` / `:model` / `n` per session. A 'session template' shortcut would let the user pick a named preset that spawns all the sessions in one keystroke (or auto-spawns on launch when a default is configured).

This is the multi-session generalization of mu-ium (which made one-at-a-time provider selection runtime-controllable).

## What

Two reasonable mechanisms, complementary not exclusive:

### A. Interactive startup picker

When mu-tui launches with no auto-template configured, show a small picker overlay:

```
  Choose a session template:
  ▶ daily       (3 sessions: opus, haiku, codex)
    review      (2 sessions: 2× sonnet)
    cheap       (1 session: haiku)
    [skip]      Start with no sessions
```

Selection spawns all the sessions in the template; user lands in F1 with them already created.

### B. Config-driven autostart

Templates live in `~/.config/mu/templates.toml` (or similar):

```toml
[templates.daily]
sessions = [
    { provider = "anthropic_api", model = "claude-opus-4-7",     name = "opus" },
    { provider = "anthropic_api", model = "claude-haiku-4-5...", name = "haiku" },
    { provider = "openai_codex",  model = "gpt-5.5",             name = "codex" },
]

[startup]
default_template = "daily"   # auto-spawn on launch
# default_template = ""        # show picker instead
```

A CLI flag overrides: `mu-tui --template review` (and a corresponding `--no-auto` or `--template -` to skip).

### Optional session naming

Templates can name their sessions (the `name` field above). When set, the session list (F1 / F3 picker) shows the name alongside the session_id — much friendlier than 'session-1, session-2, session-3' when you're juggling 3+.

## Cross-references

- mu-ium (closed): runtime :provider / :model commands. This bead extends to multi-session, presets.
- mu-cha (open, P2): F2 session tree. The picker variant could be folded into F2's session-management surface.
- mu-h04 / mu-wd2 (closed): TUI input ergonomics. Same author-of-frustration ('I do this every day, why can't it remember').

## Acceptance

- mu-tui launches with `--template daily` and creates the sessions listed in that template.
- If no template flag and config has `default_template`, that template auto-spawns.
- If neither, an interactive picker appears (or, if config disables it, the existing 'empty F1' state).
- Optional names for sessions are honored when set.
- Bad template (missing API key for a referenced provider) gracefully skips that session with a firehose warning, doesn't abort the whole template.

title: Implement mu-038 FileBackend — cross-daemon same-machine discovery
description:
Spec at specs/mu-038-projection-queries-and-discovery.md (jj qrpzxpuq 1af4f26a).

FileBackend writes TOML files under ~/.local/share/mu/daemons/<daemon_id>/sessions/<session_id>.toml on announce, deletes on withdraw. session.list with include_remote=true enumerates files across all daemon directories.

Liveness via daemon.heartbeat file mtime — daemons touch it every N seconds. Readers treat sessions from a daemon with stale heartbeat (>30s) as 'may be dead'.

Cross-daemon trust is OUT of scope here — same-user-on-same-machine assumed. Auth comes later (likely biscuit-auth).

title: Future: readonly / inspection mode for mutating tools
description:
Add a readonly/inspection run mode. Agents may perform read-only work autonomously, but write/edit/bash/network/delegate/mutating actions pause at the exact proposed tool call for human inspection.

Approval UI should show session/task ownership, proposed args, paths/commands, diff/impact preview where available, and choices: allow once, allow scope/pattern for this session, deny call, cancel tool, cancel session/delegate, or quiesce all.

Composes with session.input_required, tool policy, and emergency cancel-only mode.

Related memory: 73505005.

title: Future: emergency stop / cancel-only control plane
description:
Design and implement explicit operator controls for runaway/unsafe agent work.

Modes to support eventually:
- normal
- readonly / inspection
- cancel-only / quiesce: no new model calls, tool calls, or delegates; only reduce exposure / summarize / stop
- abort / evict all: cancel active tool calls, stop delegates, terminate subprocess groups/jails
- hard stop / recovery: immediate kill with explicit reconciliation on restart

Dangerous broad actions should use guarded-trigger UX: show affected sessions/tools/delegates, require typed nonce confirmation, optionally reason/operator, and emit durable audit events. Inspired by industrial emergency-stop controls and matching-engine cancel-only / order-eviction controls.

Related memories: 1440515e, 516fedc7, 0d326bed, c8473c42.

title: AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy (mu-ktq follow-on)
description:
Follow-on to mu-ktq (foundational traits, closed). Provide the Anthropic-specific implementations of the new traits introduced in crates/mu-core/src/context/.

## Scope

In:
- `AnthropicProviderRenderer` impl of `ProviderRenderer` — render a RetainedRope into Anthropic-shaped Messages API request payload (system prompt + messages array, content blocks). Honor `ProjectionTarget::AgentView` (full fidelity for the model); operator-view rendering can be added later.
- `AnthropicCacheStrategy` impl of `CacheStrategy` — place a single cache boundary at the last stable+cacheable span position (per spec lines 567-578); annotate that message with `CacheMarker::Ephemeral`; the Anthropic adapter translates that into `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` at the boundary message.
- Tests: at least one rope shape with a clear stable/volatile split; verify boundary placement; verify annotation lands on the right message.

Out:
- Adoption in the live agent loop (separate bead — see "live-loop adoption").
- OpenAI-specific rendering (separate bead).
- Full RetainedRope (= mu-nat).

## Design questions to resolve before implementing

1. Coexistence with existing mu-i6j cache_control work — where does the current `cache_control` annotation get applied today? Does the new strategy replace that code path, or does it sit alongside until the adoption bead lands?
2. The current Anthropic adapter takes `&[AgentMessage]` (mu-core/src/agent/provider.rs:62). Does the new renderer return ProviderMessages and a separate adapter step converts to the Anthropic wire JSON, or should AnthropicProviderRenderer go all the way to the wire shape?
3. System-prompt placement: Anthropic has a top-level `system` field (mu-n48), distinct from messages. Where does the system-role span fall in the rendered output?

## Acceptance

Trait impls compile and link against existing mu-ai Anthropic code paths; tests verify boundary placement; cargo test --workspace green; bead-close commit with file:line citations.

## References

- mu-ktq (closed; foundational traits)
- specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md:564-612
- mu-i6j (existing cache_control work)
- mu-n48 (system prompt support)

title: Cluster-service spec (mu-040): cross-machine federation
description:
Per memory d32898bd. Cross-machine mu visibility goes through a SEPARATE cluster-service process, not via etcd-backed-into-each-daemon.

Service responsibilities:
- Accept client connections (TUI, web UI, agents)
- Authentication / authorization
- Use etcd to enumerate sessions across all daemons in the cluster
- Aggregate cross-daemon mailbox routing

Daemons stay narrow: they just announce themselves + sessions to etcd via a thin SessionDiscovery::EtcdAnnouncerBackend (or similar). The aggregation happens elsewhere.

Future spec — write when we have a real driver for cross-machine work. Likely paired with biscuit-auth for the trust layer.

title: TUI: pagination + scrolling for long transcripts
description:
session.events supports after_event_id pagination. The transcript pane currently pulls limit=500 from event 0 and renders all of it without scrollback. For long sessions (>500 events) we'd miss the end; for very long ones the render would be slow.

Add j/k or PageUp/PageDown to scroll the transcript. Lazy-load via after_event_id when scrolling past the loaded window.

title: mu-ovl-B: Implement OperatorView rendering rules per SpanKind
description:
Sub-bead of mu-ovl. Behavior phase. Replaces the shallow OperatorView prefix-tag at `renderer.rs:224` with per-SpanKind rendering rules that make AgentView and OperatorView visibly different.

## Scope

IN: in `FauxProviderRenderer::render_content` (`renderer.rs:221+`), match on `SpanKind` and produce distinct OperatorView output for each variant. Suggested rules (autonomous session may refine — record final choices in commit body):

| SpanKind | AgentView | OperatorView |
|---|---|---|
| System / User / Assistant | full content | full content |
| ToolResult | full content (raw JSON or text) | one-line structured summary — e.g., `[tool-result: ok, 3 items]` or first N chars |
| ToolCall | full content (name + args JSON) | name + arg keys only — e.g., `call: bash(command)` |
| ToolSchema | full schema JSON | name only — e.g., `[tool: bash]` |
| SkillActivation | full activation event | `[skill: <name>]` badge |
| MemoryInjection | full memory content | `<memory id=X collapsed>...</memory>` collapsible tag |
| Compaction | full synthetic span content | `[compacted N spans: <summary>]` |
| FileLoad | full file content | `[file: <path>] <N lines>` |

Add a `SpanKindMetadata` or inline policy table in renderer.rs documenting the rules.

Tests: hand-build a rope containing at least one span of each new kind, render under both targets, assert different output structures. The 'same input, two outputs' contrast is the load-bearing demonstration of the projection abstraction.

OUT: TUI integration (consuming OperatorView output in mu-pex / mu-jb3). File a follow-on bead `mu-ovl-tui` blocked-by mu-ovl-B for that work.

## Acceptance

- Per-SpanKind rule landed for all new variants
- Test asserting AgentView != OperatorView output for at least 4 variants
- Test asserting AgentView output is unchanged for the 4 original variants
- `cargo test --workspace` green at commit
- Commit body documents the final per-variant rule choices (if they diverged from this table)
- Follow-on bead `mu-ovl-tui` filed for TUI consumption

## References

- Existing renderer impl: `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:215-235` (where render_content lives)
- Spec lines 614-644 (agent-view vs operator-view projections)
- Experiment doc: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-2026-05-13d-mu-ovl.md`

title: Adopt ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy in live agent loop (mu-ktq follow-on)
description:
Final follow-on for the mu-ktq trait suite. Wire the new `ProviderRenderer` + `CacheStrategy` traits into the live agent loop so the rope-based context substrate replaces the current `&[AgentMessage]` flow.

## Blocked by

- mu-bn4 (AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy) — needs the Anthropic impls before the loop can switch over.
- Likely also mu-nat (full RetainedRope) — the stub used by mu-ktq is not load-bearing enough for the live loop. The full rope is needed so events accumulate into the retained pointer set rather than being assembled into a Vec<AgentMessage> ad-hoc.

## Scope

In:
- Replace the loop's per-turn `Vec<AgentMessage>` assembly with a `RetainedRope` projected through the appropriate `ProviderRenderer`.
- Plumb `CacheStrategy` selection (per-session config: NoCacheStrategy vs AnthropicCacheStrategy based on provider).
- Emit a `ContextAssembly` event (per spec lines 167-228) recording the rope state and the renderer/strategy used.
- Tests: end-to-end loop test that asserts the same model-visible payload comes out of the new path as the old.

Out:
- Operator-view consumers (`mu-ovl`) — separate bead.
- Subagent handoff via shared event log (`mu-x9j`) — separate bead.

## Acceptance

Live loop uses ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy; cargo test --workspace green; integration test confirms no regression in produced provider payloads; spec-amend commit if the wire/RPC surface shifts at all.

## References

- mu-ktq (closed; foundational traits)
- mu-bn4 (Anthropic impls; blocks this)
- mu-nat (full RetainedRope; blocks this)
- specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md

title: TUI: short prompt gets obscured by overlapping UI element
description:
## Why

Reported by user, 2026-05-11: "when typing a prompt over a short sentence, it gets obscured by the tooltip."

The prompt input field overlaps with another rendered element (the user said "tooltip" — could be the F-key tab row, the firehose, the statusline, or an overlay). Net effect: typed text becomes hard to read while in input mode.

## What I don't know yet

- Which element is doing the overlapping (need a screenshot or terminal-size repro).
- At what terminal sizes it manifests (probably narrow heights — the firehose / statusline competing with the input area for the same rows).
- Whether the overlap is z-order (one widget drawing over another) or layout (Constraints summing to more than the available area).

## What

After repro:
- If z-order: re-order the render calls so the input field is the last thing drawn.
- If layout: the Layout::default().constraints([...]) somewhere is summing to too much for short terminals; need a min-height check on the input row, or a graceful collapse of one of the other rows.
- If overlay/popup: the popup probably draws on top of the input field; should auto-dismiss when input mode is entered.

## Acceptance

- Reproduce the obscuring at a known terminal size.
- Fix the responsible widget interaction.
- Add a small comment near the fix explaining what was overlapping, so future renderer changes don't re-introduce it.

## Cross-reference

- mu-wd2 (arrow keys) — same area of code; consider tackling together since both touch input-mode rendering.

title: Profile-driven file-edit isolation (generalize worktree isolation pattern)
description:
Generalizes claude-code 2.1.139's background-task worktree-isolation pattern into mu's session-profile model. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

claude-code 2.1.139 isolates background-task file edits to a private worktree, automatically. mu's equivalent should NOT be hard-wired to one task shape (background-only); it should be a property of the session's profile / authority tier. A "Restricted" or "Delegate" profile MAY require an isolated working surface; a "Root" profile does not.

## Scope

In:
- SessionProfile struct gains `file_isolation: FileIsolation { None | Worktree { root: PathBuf } | ReadOnly }`.
- Tool-side enforcement: Edit / Write tools consult the active session's `file_isolation` before resolving paths.
- Default: profiles inherit `None`; the Delegate / Restricted tiers (from mu-ikz, mu-pex) override to `Worktree { ... }`.

Out:
- The worktree-creation lifecycle itself (assume operator-side `jj new`-equivalent).
- Cross-session merge semantics (a follow-up concern once we observe usage).

## Acceptance

* `FileIsolation` type lives in mu-core; serialized in Capability/Profile.
* Edit/Write tools honor isolation; tests cover the three variants.
* Default-tier sessions are unaffected (None ⇒ existing behavior).

## References

- Refines: mu-627
- Memory: `fb5cd5be`
- Companion: `mu-ikz` (authority-coloring; the tier model that drives profile defaults).

title: Wire cache_control markers on system prompt + tool definitions in mu-ai anthropic provider
description:
## Why

mu-pex F5 dashboard hand-test (2026-05-11) confirmed cache% = 0% on a real anthropic-api+haiku run:

- 51,388 input tokens accumulated across 14 tool calls + 1 final response
- 0 cache_read_input_tokens
- 0 cache_creation_input_tokens

The Usage struct (mu-core/src/agent/types.rs) and the dashboard already *report* cache metrics — but mu isn't *marking* prompt content for caching, so the API never caches.

At haiku pricing ($0.80/M input, $0.08/M cached-read), that ask cost ~$0.04 instead of the ~$0.005 it would have cost if the system prompt + tool defs were cached. ~85% savings on a representative mu workload, plus a 30-50% latency reduction on the prompt-processing portion of TTFT.

## What

Add Anthropic-style `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` markers on the long-lived parts of the request body:

1. System prompt (one marker at the end of the system text block)
2. Tool definitions (one marker on the last tool spec, which Anthropic interprets as 'cache everything up to and including this')

These don't need per-call configurability — they're the parts of the request that are stable across asks within a session. The variable parts (per-turn user messages, tool_results) stay uncached.

Anthropic supports up to 4 cache_control breakpoints per request. We use 2; that leaves headroom for future cacheable segments (e.g., a chunked conversation prefix once context windows grow).

## Where

`crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs` (and possibly `anthropic_tests.rs`).

## How to A/B test

This bead is uniquely testable against the work we just landed: run F5 dashboard against this branch and against main, compare the `cache%` column after a session with multiple asks against the same prompt. Expected: 0% on main, 30-90% on this branch depending on how much of the request is the stable prefix vs. per-turn content.

## Acceptance

- Workspace tests still pass.
- Anthropic provider unit tests assert cache_control markers are present on system prompt + tool defs in the request body.
- F5 dashboard cache% column shows nonzero on a multi-ask session against anthropic_api.

## References

- mu-pex F5 dashboard: commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23
- Anthropic prompt caching docs (external): https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching
- Discovered during 2026-05-11 hand-test (51k input tokens, 0% cache hit)

title: TUI: implement F9 mailbox view

title: Authority-coloring as refinement on top of state-coloring (mu-pex)
description:
Refines mu-pex's per-session state-coloring model with an authority/role layer. Memory `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 → mu mapping) and memory `5ff7511e` (color palette stays coarse — ~3 colors, never more than 5).

## Motivation

mu-pex's state-coloring distinguishes session states (idle / streaming / tool-running / done). claude-code 2.1.139's role/tier concept layers on top: which AUTHORITY does a session carry (root capability, attenuated user capability, delegate capability)? These two axes are independent — a streaming root-authority session looks different from a streaming attenuated-delegate session.

## Scope

In:
- Authority tier enum (Root / User / Delegate / Restricted). 3–4 tiers max — coarse per `5ff7511e`.
- Tier rendered as a coloring overlay (border color, label) ON TOP of the state coloring. Two visual axes: state (fill/animation) + authority (border/label).
- Wiring: SessionStatusSummary gains `authority_tier` derived from `Capability`. TUI list view picks up the tier.

Out:
- Authority tier transitions (delegation already handles this).
- Beyond-coarse palettes (memory `5ff7511e` caps at ~5 colors).

## Acceptance

* `Capability` → `AuthorityTier` projection function with unit tests.
* TUI session list shows authority tier visually distinct from state.
* No more than 4 authority tiers; rationale documented.

## References

- Refines: mu-pex
- Memory: `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping), `5ff7511e` (coarse palette)

title: TUI: expose provider/model selection in 'n' new-session flow
description:
## Why

Sessions in mu's architecture are independent — each carries its own ProviderSelector at create_session time. So having multiple concurrent sessions in one daemon, each with a different provider+model, is already supported wire-side. But the TUI's 'n' (new session) keybind always uses self.default_provider, which is a single tuple set at startup.

Net effect: a feature mu already supports is locked behind a CLI flag (--provider / --model on mu-tui launch) instead of being an in-TUI affordance.

## What

Options, in increasing scope:

A. Palette commands `:provider <kind>` and `:model <id>` that update default_provider for the next 'n' press. Smallest change; trades discoverability for terseness. ~30 LOC.

B. Two-step 'n' flow: 'n' opens a small picker (provider on top, model below, both with keyboard navigation), then create_session fires on Enter. ~80-150 LOC depending on picker polish.

C. Both: palette for keyboard-fast use; picker for discoverability.

Recommend (A) first — it gets the feature usable in ~30 LOC; (B) can layer on later when the rest of the TUI gets palette-/picker-friendlier.

## Acceptance

- Running mu-tui without --provider/--model, then issuing :provider openai_codex / :model gpt-5.5 in the palette, then pressing 'n', creates a session against openai_codex/gpt-5.5.
- Existing default-provider behavior preserved when no palette command is issued.
- Session list (F2 when it lands, or the session row in F1) shows which provider+model each session ran against.

## References

- Default flow today: crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::App::create_session uses self.default_provider.
- ProviderSelector wire type: crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs::ProviderSelector.

title: Implement mu-035: session.provider_status + cancel_outstanding
description:
Spec at specs/mu-035-session-provider-status.md (jj spouxlsp d69719c3).

Three deliverables:
1. session.provider_status periodic notification with kind (awaiting_first_token | streaming | thinking | tool_executing | awaiting_tool_result | idle), started_at_unix_ms, elapsed_ms, optional bytes_received / tool_call_id.
2. session.cancel_outstanding narrow cancel (kills provider call, session stays alive).
3. daemon.outstanding_calls fleet view.

INV-4 is load-bearing: the emit-tick runs on a tokio timer independent of the provider future, so a stalled provider still produces visible status.

Once landed:
- TUI's phase line gets authoritative ground truth (no more session.list-derived heuristics)
- mu_client.py defensive layer becomes belt-and-suspenders
- Refinement experiment's silent stalls become instantly diagnosable

Estimated 1 week of focused Rust work.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Phase A landed in jj okqunkry ef544959 — emits ProviderStatus on state transitions (AwaitingFirstToken / Streaming / ToolExecuting). Wire types for cancel_outstanding + daemon.outstanding_calls also added. Remaining: Phase B periodic timer (INV-4 — load-bearing), Phase C session.cancel_outstanding RPC handler, Phase D daemon.outstanding_calls handler.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Phase B landed in jj yznoysqw 28ace52b — periodic emit every 1000ms via tokio::time::interval, INV-4 (load-bearing) now actually holds. TUI's phase line can now render 'awaiting first token 4.2s' or 'tool: read 8.1s' with growing elapsed_ms. Phase C (session.cancel_outstanding RPC handler) and Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls) still to go.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Phase C landed in jj lmoxwqvs 7733782d — session.cancel_outstanding RPC. AgentInput::CancelOutstanding + Outcome::OutstandingCancelled sentinel; dispatch handler in handle_cancel_outstanding; abort propagates through stream/tool/approval recv sites; outer loop catches and emits Done(Aborted), keeps session alive. was_in field is placeholder until Phase D plumbs the live tracker.

Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls fleet view) is the last remaining sub-issue — requires promoting the per-session ProviderStatusTracker state into the Sessions registry so dispatch can read it without going through the agent loop's stack frame.

title: AskSessionRequest: per-ask provider/model override
description:
## Why

Today, a session's (provider, model) is fixed at create_session time. To use a different model for one ask — e.g., "use haiku for this cheap subtask in the middle of a sonnet session" — there's no path short of creating a new session (losing context).

Surfaced 2026-05-11 during a multi-iteration discussion about model routing. The agent-router script (~/src/claude-personal/scripts/agent-router) already does this externally; doing it natively in mu means a session can mix model choices without going through a wrapper.

## What

Add an optional `provider_override` field to AskSessionRequest. When present, the agent loop builds the provider for THIS ask using the override instead of the session's stored provider, but doesn't persist it — the session keeps its original (provider, model). Subsequent asks use the session default again unless overridden.

The 'persistent swap' variant is its own bead (see mu-??? session.set_provider) — this one is per-call only.

## Wire shape (proposal)

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "user_message": "...",
  "provider_override": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001" }
}
```

## Considerations

- Cost: opens the door to 'route each ask to the cheapest viable model' patterns. This is what the agent-router script does today externally.
- Capability surface: must validate that the override provider supports the tools the session has bound; otherwise error early.
- Cache impact: overriding the model means the previously-cached prefix (system prompt + tools) becomes a cache miss on the override target. The dashboard's cache% column will reflect this naturally.

## Acceptance

- Unit test: AskSessionRequest with provider_override hits the override provider, not the session's stored one.
- The override doesn't mutate session state — subsequent asks without override use the session's original (provider, model).
- F5 dashboard shows the override as its own (provider, model) row (since the daemon.usage_history aggregator groups by provider_kind + model on each call, this should fall out naturally).

title: F5 dashboard: add tool_total_ms p95 column
description:
## Why

The mu-pex Phase 1 RPC (`daemon.usage_history`) returns `tool_total_ms` (per-(ToolCall, ToolResult) pair durations, aggregated as median + p95) in every row, but the F5 dashboard's Phase A renderer (mu-xln, commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23) omits this column. Discovered 2026-05-11 when a 29s/14-tool ask showed 17s of streaming + 116 output tokens — most of the wall was tool execution, and the dashboard couldn't show it.

"Where is the wall time going?" is one of the canonical metrics-dashboard questions. tool_total_ms is the answer for tool-heavy workloads and TTFT / streaming together are the answer for prose-heavy ones. Without the tool column, the dashboard only answers half the question.

## What

In crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs render_usage:

1. Add a 'tool p95' column header between 'wall p95' and 'in tok'.
2. Pull row["tool_total_ms"]["p95"] and format via the existing fmt_ms_p95 helper.
3. Update the widths array to include the new column (8 char width fits 'XXXXms' + spacing).
4. Verify the table still fits in a standard 120-col terminal (current rendered width is ~106 chars + 2 borders; adding 8+1 = ~115, fits).

## Acceptance

- F5 pane shows a 'tool p95' column.
- Empty rows (no tool calls) render '—' or '0ms' via fmt_ms_p95 (it returns '—' on None).
- No layout regression on narrow terminals (test at 80, 100, 120 cols).

## Note on column ordering

Current order favors timing-decomposition reading left to right: ttft p95 → stream p95 → wall p95. Inserting tool p95 between stream and wall preserves that 'what happened during the wall' progression: AwaitingFirstToken time → streaming time → tool time → total wall.

title: Pluggable ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy traits
description:
Introduce two orthogonal traits for the rope-based context architecture:

```rust
trait ProviderRenderer { fn render(rope: &RetainedRope, target: ProjectionTarget) -> ProviderMessages; }
trait CacheStrategy { fn boundaries(rope) -> Vec<CacheBoundary>; fn annotate(messages, boundaries); }
```

ProviderRenderer is per-provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, FauxProvider). CacheStrategy is composable: where to put cache boundaries given the rope. Multiple strategies coexist for A/B testing.

## Scope

In: trait defs in crates/mu-core; AnthropicCacheStrategy (boundary at first volatile retained span; cache_control: ephemeral); NoCacheStrategy (no-op for OpenAI/Faux); AnthropicProviderRenderer; tests.

Out: A/B-testing harness; OpenAI impl; adoption in live agent loop (later bead).

## Why

Provider differences (cache, message format, system prompt placement) are scattered. Centralizing behind two traits makes: adding a provider mechanical; cache strategies A/B testable; rope semantics provider-independent.

## Acceptance

Traits compile with at least one impl each; existing Anthropic paths refactored without regression; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-2-provider-cache-strategies.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8
Memories: 3cc7a18c, fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 validation)
Existing: mu-i6j (cache_control work)
close_reason: Foundational half landed (ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy traits + stub RetainedRope + NoCacheStrategy + FauxProviderRenderer + 17 tests). Provider-specific impls (Anthropic, OpenAI) deferred to follow-on beads.

title: Implement mu-037 phase 1: single-daemon peer.hello + mailbox
description:
Spec at specs/mu-037-peer-discovery-mailbox.md (jj ozyprmqn cc380197). Phase 1 only — single-daemon scope.

Two delegate sessions in the same daemon can exchange messages without going through the parent. Wire surface:
- peer.hello / peer.reply with capability advertisement + negotiation
- mailbox.post / mailbox.list / mailbox.consume
- New MailboxMessage event-log entries

Reuses mu-033 Capability entirely — peer handles ARE attenuated capabilities. No new authorization machinery.

Estimated ~1 week. Phase 2 (cross-daemon same-machine via unix socket + filesystem registry) is a follow-up issue.

title: ContextAssembly v2: per-segment source-map
description:
Per specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md. ContextAssembly v1 records counts + provider info; v2 records the full source-map: for each message in the assembled prompt, which source event(s) it came from and what range.

Enables 'why was this in context' (the F4 Context Inspector view from mu-ui-mockups-2026-05-10.md).

Bigger lift — needs schema work + agent loop participation to actually track the source-event mapping during assembly. Write when the inspector UI work creates real demand.

title: Session persistence across daemon restart
description:
Today sessions live in mu serve's in-memory Sessions map. When mu serve exits (TUI quits → stdin closes → transport exits → sessions drops), every session and its event log are lost.

For a daily-driver TUI that's used across many work sessions, that's a real limitation: you can't 'come back to' the conversation you had yesterday.

What's needed:
  - Event-log persistence (SQLite or JSONL — the event_log.rs design doc references both)
  - SessionDiscovery (mu-038) backend that reads the persisted logs on startup, resurrects SessionInfo entries (the agent loop doesn't need to be re-spawned for a 'cold' session — it spawns on the next ask_session)
  - Cap on retention (per-session age limit? per-session size limit? user choice)
  - Capability state persistence (a session with attenuated capability should keep that on cold reload)

Mentioned as deferred work in mu-036 spec (autonomous loops). Write its own spec when implementation pressure builds — likely after mu-035 Phase B/C/D and mu-037 phase 1 are done.

Workaround for now: keep mu serve running detached; have the TUI connect-to-existing rather than spawn-new. That's not implemented but is a much smaller piece of work than full persistence.

title: Thread system_prompt from CreateSessionRequest to Provider::stream
description:
## Why

`CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt: Option<String>` exists at the wire protocol layer (crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs:66) but is **accepted at the API boundary and silently dropped**. The Provider::stream trait signature only takes (messages, tools); no system prompt parameter. So clients can send the field expecting customization and get no effect.

Surfaced 2026-05-11 while implementing mu-i6j (cache_control markers on anthropic provider request). The implementation comments explicitly note this gap:

> System prompt is the other classic cache target but mu doesn't currently thread `system_prompt` from CreateSessionRequest through to Provider::stream — see protocol.rs:66 for the wire field; the body construction does not include a `system` key. When that gap is closed, add a second cache_control marker on the last system content block.

See commit ykumwozs 77bb0ce4.

## What

1. Plumb `system_prompt: Option<String>` from the session manager into AgentLoop / Provider invocations. Existing call sites: dispatch::handle_create_session, sessions storage, agent loop's call-out to the provider.
2. Extend Provider::stream trait signature with system_prompt parameter, OR store it on the AgentConfig (preferred — keeps the trait stable and lets each provider decide how to render it).
3. In anthropic provider's build_request_body: if system_prompt is Some, include a top-level `system` field with a content-block array containing the prompt + cache_control marker (mirroring how tools are cached in mu-i6j).
4. Other providers (openai_codex, openrouter, faux) get equivalent treatment.

## Acceptance

- A session created with system_prompt: Some("...") sees the prompt reach the LLM (verifiable by asking 'what's your system prompt' and seeing the contents).
- For anthropic_api, the F5 dashboard's cache% column rises further (system prompt joins tools as a cached prefix).
- Workspace tests still pass; new tests assert the wire-level translation.

## Out of scope

- Designing what mu's default system prompt should be. (Today's behavior is "no system prompt"; this bead preserves that as the default by treating None == today's behavior.)

title: Skills and tool schemas as rope spans (eliminate parallel loading mechanisms)
description:
Refactor skill activation and tool registration to emit spans into the rope rather than maintain separate loaded-state structures.

## Scope

In: SkillManager emits skill.activated { skill_id, span_refs } events; ToolRegistry represents each tool's schema as an addressable span; capability attenuation becomes a pointer-set filter; RetainedRope API gains activate_skill / deactivate_skill / filter_tools / provenance.

Out: span granularity policy (start per-file, refine later); skill activation triggers (keep current); operator-view UI changes (handled in mu-NEW-4).

## Why

Today skill loading and tool registration are separate code paths from context management. Unifying them: activation/deactivation IS pointer-set membership change. Subagent capability filtering reuses existing rope machinery; provenance built in.

## Acceptance

Skill-activation behavior preserved; tool schemas render identically at agent-view; capability attenuation produces observable pointer-set diff; provenance lookup works; tests for activate/deactivate, filtered tool sets, span provenance; workspace tests green.

title: TUI: suppress empty 'assistant' block on tool-only turns
description:
Currently when the model emits a turn that is entirely tool calls (no text content blocks), the transcript renders a 'assistant — (no text in this turn)' block above the magenta tool block. That's cosmetic cruft.

Fix in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::render_transcript_lines: when an 'assistant_message_event' has empty joined text AND there's a 'tool_call' or 'tool_result' event in the same logical turn (i.e. next significant event in the log is a tool_call before the next done), skip rendering the empty assistant block entirely. title: Agent-view vs operator-view projections from the rope
description:
Introduce ProjectionTarget enum (AgentView | OperatorView) and two distinct rendering paths from the same retained pointer set. ProviderRenderer takes a target.

## Scope

In: ProjectionTarget enum; OperatorViewRenderer trait; per-span-kind operator-view rules (tool result -> structured table; skill activation -> one-line badge; tool schema -> name; memory injection -> collapsible); TUI changes (mu-pex / mu-jb3) consume operator-view rendering; tests for both targets.

Out: drill-down UI for inspecting agent-view at specific turn; operator-view variations (compact/detailed/debug) — start with one canonical view.

## Why

LLM tools conflate agent view and operator view. Humans and models have different attention budgets and relevance criteria. Separating projections: operator gets usable surface (skimmable tool results, collapsible context); agent gets full fidelity; debugging becomes possible (AgentView at turn N).

## Acceptance

AgentView produces output identical to current agent context rendering; OperatorView produces distinct usable rendering; TUI integrates (at least mu-pex system_prompt indicator sources operator-view); provenance preserved for span-click drill-down; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-4-projection-targets.md

title: Add mu release binary to stable PATH (or symlink) for agent-router codex backend
description:
## Why

Overnight session tried to delegate mu-036 spec analysis to GPT via `agent-router --auth codex-oauth` (per the routing policy that says 'lean on GPT for spec-heavy tasks'). The router's codex backend expects `mu` to be on PATH for its mu_client integration. mu's release binary lives at `~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu` but isn't symlinked or PATH-exposed.

Prepending PATH inline (`PATH="$HOME/src/public_github/mu/target/release:$PATH" agent-router ...`) works one-shot but doesn't survive the next shell or programmatic invocation from a script that doesn't think to set PATH.

## What

Two reasonable options:
1. Symlink the release binary: `ln -sf ~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu ~/.local/bin/mu`. Lives alongside claude-code at `~/.local/bin` (per memory `reference_claude_binary.md` pattern). Risk: rebuilding the workspace overwrites the binary in-place, so the symlink stays valid — but a `cargo clean` breaks it. Acceptable; rerunning `cargo build --release` restores it.
2. Add the path to zsh PATH via `~/.zsh/zenvironment` (the canonical PATH-setup source per the secrets-config layout we already use). Always works; doesn't need a re-symlink after `cargo clean`.

I'd go with (2) — it's the same pattern `tq` and other build-time-installed binaries use already.

## Acceptance

`mu serve --help` works from a fresh terminal without PATH manipulation. `agent-router --auth codex-oauth ...` works without the user prepending PATH. The session-router falls back gracefully (already does — errors say 'No such file or directory: mu') if the binary is genuinely missing.

title: Metrics framework: per-call time decomposition + quality proxies
description:
Design + implementation arc for mu's ongoing observability. Came out of refinement-experiment discussion 2026-05-11: per-request hard metrics vs per-request quality metrics are different beasts, and we should be deliberate about which we track and why.

Captured in commit/results doc: refinement arm 0/3 forbidden vs baseline 3/3, at 2.2x wall time. That cost is fine for delegation-style work but expensive for interactive — we want to be able to SEE the cost decomposition (TTFT, streaming, tool time, thinking) rather than just total wall.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Two classes of metrics, very different to track
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Hard metrics (always trackable, no ground truth):
  - TTFT = AwaitingFirstToken → Streaming transition (mu-035)
  - Streaming duration
  - ToolExecuting duration (sum)
  - Wall end-to-end
  - Tokens in/out, cache hit %, tool count
  - Per-provider error rate

These don't tell us correctness but they DO catch drift: median TTFT per provider per hour would catch 'codex is stalled' in minutes instead of the 14h silence we had on 2026-05-10/11.

Quality metrics (need fixture):
  - Success rate vs ground truth
  - Refinement passes to clean
  - Cost-to-correct = total tokens to 1 successful response

These need a benchmark fixture (like the spec-summary task in protocol-eval). Outside fixtures, every request is unique.

Proxy metrics (work without ground truth):
  - Refinement-fire rate (if we run grader on everything)
  - User-marked retry rate (TUI accept/reject affordance)
  - Tool error rate (model-induced vs system-induced)

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Implementation arc
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Now-easy (post mu-035 A/B/C + mu-038):
  - daemon.usage_history projection RPC returning median/p95 of
    TTFT / streaming_duration / tool_duration / wall, grouped by
    (provider, model, time-bucket).
  - Backed by aggregating ProviderStatus events from the event log
    (or live tracker snapshots once tracker is in Sessions — Phase D).

Soonish (mu-xln existing P2 bead — F5 Usage Dashboard):
  - Renders the daemon.usage_history projection.
  - Per-provider/model rows with TTFT p95, streaming duration p95,
    tool-call-count distribution.
  - Cost estimate column once we have a per-provider price table.

Later (quality proxies):
  - TUI affordance: '👍 helpful' / '👎 not what I wanted' / 'try again'
    after each session.done.
  - Capture in event log (new EventPayload variant: UserFeedback).
  - Surface as a quality-proxy column in F5.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Cross-references
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Depends on:
  - mu-iwq Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls + ProviderStatusTracker
    in Sessions) — for snapshot enumeration. Phase A/B/C already
    give us the per-call event stream.
  - mu-038 projection-query patterns — same code area, same style.

Composes with:
  - mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — its data source.
  - mu-036 autonomous loop — refinement-fire-rate is a great
    quality proxy for autonomous sessions.

Not in scope here:
  - Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens). Should be its
    own small bead because it's an updated-rarely lookup table.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

When to implement: not blocking daily-driver. Worth picking up
after mu-iwq Phase D lands or as a parallel slice if a slow
provider catches our attention again.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Per-model corrections rate is a great proxy metric to add to the design — break down refinement_fire_rate by (provider, model) and you get a comparative quality fingerprint. Different models have different priors (today's experiment confirmed gpt-5.5 has a strong login/logout prior; haiku-4.5 may have different ones). Tracking corrections_per_call by model surfaces:
  - which models are best for which task types (factual extraction, code review, etc.)
  - whether a model is degrading over time (vendor pushed an update? prior drift?)
  - cost-of-correctness tradeoff (cheaper-but-more-corrections vs more-expensive-but-fewer)

Add to the F5 dashboard sketch: per-(provider, model) row showing corrections_per_100_calls when grader was enabled. Surfaces 'haiku gets 5/100 wrong on summarisation; gpt-5.5 gets 33/100; sonnet gets 2/100' or whatever the truth turns out to be.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1 (A-scope) landed in commit rwyyuout 28c677c6 (`feat(mu-pex): daemon.usage_history projection RPC — Phase 1 (A-scope)`).

What landed:
- New RPC `daemon.usage_history` with (since_unix_ms?, until_unix_ms?, time_bucket_ms?) request params, returns rows grouped by (provider, model, time-bucket).
- Per-row distributions (median+p95): tool_total_ms, wall_ms, model_call_latency_ms (proxy: AssistantMessageEvent.ts − ContextAssembly.ts).
- Per-row sums: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, reasoning_tokens, tool_call_count.
- Per-row counts: session_count, error_count.
- Aggregates over in-memory sessions visible via `sessions.snapshot_for_listing()`.
- 12 new unit tests; full workspace test suite green (289 passed, 1 ignored).

Explicitly None in Phase 1 (will land in Phase 1.5 / Iteration 2):
- `ttft_ms` — needs durable `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` (currently wire-only mu-035).
- `streaming_ms` — same reason.

Phase 1.5 plan (queued in task list):
1. Add `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs).
2. Extend `forwarder.rs::to_log_event` to map `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant alongside the existing wire-side mapping.
3. Update `payload_kind_str` in dispatch.rs and the TUI's sidechannel branch in render_transcript_lines.
4. Extend `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` to compute TTFT (AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition) and streaming_ms (time spent in Streaming per call).
5. Populate the two Option<PercentileStats> fields in UsageHistoryRow.

Still out of scope at the bead level:
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of this RPC.
- UserFeedback EventPayload + TUI affordance.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead.
- Cross-daemon and disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 landed in commit lrslqwuv b0944d40 — durable ProviderStatusUpdate + TTFT/streaming populated.

Changes:
- New `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs) mirroring the wire-side `ProviderStatusEvent` minus session_id.
- forwarder `to_log_event` now maps `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant. Wire-side `translate_event` unchanged: both projections still derive from the same AgentEvent source — they no longer disagree about durability.
- payload_kind_str (dispatch) + derive_status_from_events (discovery) + render_transcript_lines sidechannel (mu-tui) all extended to handle the new variant.
- `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` now walks ProviderStatusUpdate events; consecutive transition emissions (elapsed_ms == 0) yield duration samples for the prior state. Periodic ticks (elapsed_ms > 0) are skipped — they share started_at with their opening transition.
- `aggregate_into_rows` populates `row.ttft_ms` and `row.streaming_ms` via `percentile_stats` over pooled samples. Both stay None when a group has no samples — same shape as Phase 1.

Tests added (+6 over Phase 1, total now 295 active / 1 ignored):
- extraction_computes_ttft_from_awaiting_first_token_transition
- extraction_periodic_ticks_do_not_close_a_state_period
- extraction_records_streaming_periods_separately (interrupted Streaming yields 2 samples)
- extraction_unterminated_state_period_is_dropped (in-flight or aborted)
- aggregation_populates_ttft_and_streaming_in_row
- forwarder::log_records_provider_status_update

Still queued (parking-lot, not in any commit):
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of daemon.usage_history.
- TUI feedback affordance (👍/👎/try again) + `UserFeedback` EventPayload variant — quality proxy.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead (out-of-scope per the original bead).
- Cross-daemon / disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.
- Per-comment from 2026-05-11 20:14: per-(provider, model) corrections_per_100_calls when grader enabled — needs refinement integration first.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 follow-up fix in commit sunvrnkp 2b84e8b1 — discovered via hand-test against anthropic-api+haiku.

Two bugs in the initial Phase 1.5 transition-detection logic (both in extract_per_session_metrics):

1. **The transition filter `elapsed_ms == 0` was too strict.** The agent loop emits the AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition with elapsed_ms set to call_started.elapsed() (loop_.rs:880), not 0. The filter silently dropped that emission. Net effect: `ttft_ms.count` was counting gaps *between asks* (the only AFT→AFT pair the filter saw); `streaming_ms.count` was always 0.

2. **No Done-driven close-out.** The agent loop doesn't emit a Streaming→Idle transition on session.done. One ask's open Streaming state would bleed into the inter-ask gap before the next AFT entry, polluting streaming_ms with wait time.

Fix: identify transitions by started_at_unix_ms uniqueness (ticks within a period share their period's started_at; transitions always introduce a new one); close out pending state on Done using the Done's timestamp.

Hand-test now produces credible numbers:
- ttft_ms p95 = 1184ms for haiku via anthropic-api (TTFT-dominated, as expected for tiny prompts)
- streaming_ms p95 = 62ms for 5-token responses (~10ms/token)
- wall_ms matches Done.elapsed_ms exactly

Tests added: extraction_streaming_entry_with_nonzero_elapsed_still_counts + extraction_done_closes_out_pending_state_between_asks. Workspace tests: 297 active, 1 ignored.

title: Architecture: amend event-sourced-context.md with skill/tool/cache/projection/subagent/file-watch extensions
bare-equivalent boot mode for mu (minimal-context boot for delegates and sub-sessions)
description:
Implements a minimal-context boot mode for mu, equivalent to claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` flag. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

Today every mu session boots with the full operator default: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory context injection, plugin sync, hooks. Most of this is wrong for delegate / sub-session use — a delegate that exists to answer one well-scoped question doesn't need the operator's accumulated memory or the hook configuration.

claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` mode is the same idea: a different *initial retention set* (per the rope amendment in mu-qk8). The rope architecture says this should be declarative — a profile property, not a flag.

## Scope

In:
- SessionBootMode { Full | Bare } (or as a BootProfile carrying which spans to load).
- Bare mode skips: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory injection, plugin/hook sync.
- Bare mode keeps: tool registry, capability, transport.
- Plumbing: create_session and delegate_session accept the boot mode; default is Full for explicit user-driven creates, Bare for delegates by default (with override).
- Tests: a bare session has no CLAUDE.md spans and no auto-memory in its initial retained set.

Out:
- Per-source granularity (skip CLAUDE.md but keep hooks, etc.) — start with one coarse switch; refine if usage shows it's needed.
- Operator-view UI for boot-mode selection — assume CLI / RPC param for now.

## Acceptance

* Boot mode selectable via API (create_session + delegate_session).
* Bare-mode session's initial retained span set is observably smaller (test).
* Default for delegate_session is Bare; create_session default stays Full.
* No regression in existing Full-mode behavior.

## References

- Memory: fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping)
- Architecture: rope amendment in specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md, Cache-boundary alignment section (the --bare interpretation as a different initial retention set).
- Companions: mu-ikz (authority tier), mu-hlf (file isolation) — together these three constitute the 2.1.139 profile / authority feature mapping.

title: Per-session JSONL event log on disk (write-side persistence)
description:
mu serve writes every SessionEvent to a per-session JSONL file as it's appended to the in-memory event log. No read-back-at-restart logic — that's mu-mh4 Phase 2. Write-only.

Motivation: when sharing TUI test sessions with Claude (or any other agent / human reviewer), an on-disk log is faster, more accurate (no copy-paste truncation), and structurally typed (JSONL parses, terminal screenshots don't).

Scope:
  - Path: ~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl
  - One serde-encoded SessionEvent per line
  - Append-only; no rotation in v1 (mu sessions are bounded by daemon lifetime)
  - Daemon-id directory means concurrent daemons coexist cleanly
  - Write fails do NOT block in-memory append (log fsync delay must not stall the agent loop)

Implementation:
  - SessionEventLog gains an optional disk_writer field
  - serve_with_io creates the file on session insert
  - On append(), write the encoded line; ignore IO errors (best-effort)
  - File opens for append on first event, drops on session removal

Composes with mu-mh4: this is the FOUNDATION for cold-reload. Once write-side is durable, mu-mh4 Phase 2 adds the read-at-startup loop + SessionDiscovery resurrection.

Composes with mu-pex (metrics framework): the per-session JSONL files become a natural source for offline analysis (median TTFT per provider, cost-of-correctness over time, etc.) without needing a live daemon connection.

Also useful for protocol-eval — instead of stitching events back together from notifications, the analysis layer can just walk the JSONL files.

Filed P2; small implementation (30-60 min).

title: Periodic codex smoke-test: validate mu-035 heartbeat catches backend stalls
description:
## Why

mu-035 Phase B added periodic ProviderStatusUpdate ticks while in non-streaming waits specifically so a stalled provider stays visible instead of hanging silently. The classic motivating incident was the 14hr openai-codex stall observed 2026-05-10/11 (see mu-cea). Validating that the heartbeat actually catches a recurrence — and how fast — needs running codex regularly enough to hit a stall in practice.

Filed as a behavioral / monitoring task: not new code, but a habit + a place to record observations.

## What

Use openai-codex as the periodic provider for at least 1 ask/day during normal mu testing. Watch the F5 dashboard:
- ttft p95 for openai_codex should look like other providers' (sub-second to a few seconds typical).
- If a stall is in progress, the periodic ProviderStatusUpdate ticks should keep arriving (visible in F8 firehose) even when the model isn't sending content.
- Wall p95 should be the slowest signal; if it diverges wildly from ttft+stream+tool, that's a stall signature.

If a stall recurs:
1. Reopen mu-cea (or file a new bead) with the date + duration + recovery mechanism.
2. Check whether mu-035's heartbeat fired during the stall (it should — if not, that's a separate bug).
3. Verify session.cancel_outstanding (mu-035 Phase C) works to abort cleanly.

## Cross-reference

- mu-cea: BUG / external — openai-codex backend stalls
- mu-035: provider_status heartbeat
- The heartbeat is the load-bearing safety net here; the only way to know it works is to actually use codex regularly.

title: Future: $EDITOR prompt-edit audit events
description:
When mu supports $EDITOR for full prompt editing, prefer supervisor/UI-owned editor launch rather than launching arbitrary editors inside constrained workers. The worker asks the supervisor/broker for a prompt edit; supervisor creates a controlled temp file, launches $EDITOR, reads the result, then submits finalized text.

Consider durable events:
- PromptEditRequested
- PromptEditStarted { path/editor }
- PromptEditCompleted { sha256_before, sha256_after, bytes, duration_ms }
- PromptSubmitted

This preserves provenance while avoiding giving Capsicum-constrained workers broad editor/plugin/shell authority.

Related memory: c8137625.

title: biscuit-auth integration spec — capability tokens across daemons
description:
Once mu-037 phase 2+ enables cross-daemon work, we need an auth model. The biscuit-auth / macaroon tradition argued for in specs/architecture/capability-delegation.md is the obvious direction.

Open questions:
- Issuer model (each daemon as a CA? a central authority?)
- Revocation (mu has no revocation primitive today)
- Capability serialization wire format
- Composition with mu-033 Capability — biscuit IS a capability, not a separate auth concept

Write the spec when implementation pressure arrives; for now this is a placeholder.

title: TUI: arrow keys + Home/End/Backspace-word in prompt input mode
description:
## Why

In input mode (the prompt-typing affordance), only Backspace navigates — typed characters append, Backspace deletes the last char. There's no cursor movement: you can't arrow-left to fix a typo mid-prompt; the only path is to delete-back-to-the-typo. This is functional but feels broken because users have decades of muscle memory for readline-style editing.

Reported by user, 2026-05-11: "when trying to go back to change a typo, arrow keys aren't usable, so have to delete back to typo."

## What

Wire standard readline-ish keys into the input-mode handler in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:

| Key | Action |
|---|---|
| Left / Right | Move cursor one char |
| Ctrl-Left / Ctrl-Right (or Alt-B / Alt-F) | Move cursor one word |
| Home / Ctrl-A | Move to start |
| End / Ctrl-E | Move to end |
| Ctrl-W | Delete previous word |
| Ctrl-U | Delete to start of line |
| Ctrl-K | Delete to end of line |
| Backspace | (already works) Delete previous char |
| Delete | Delete next char |

State change: replace `prompt_buffer: String` with a (String, cursor_position: usize) pair — or use a small struct. Insert / delete operations now respect the cursor position.

Rendering: show a cursor caret at the current position in the input field (ratatui's `Span::styled(" ", inverted)` for a block cursor, or use the terminal's native cursor positioning).

## Scope

NOT in scope here (separate bead): full hx integration via Ctrl-X Ctrl-E. That's its own bead (TUI: $EDITOR integration). This bead is the basic-readline-in-place fix.

## Acceptance

- Typing 'helo' then Left, Left, Left, 'l' yields 'hello'.
- Ctrl-A jumps to start of buffer; End jumps to end.
- Ctrl-W deletes the last whitespace-delimited word.
- Cursor visibly tracks position (block cursor, blinking or static).

## Estimate

~50-80 LOC in the input-mode handler + a small struct for cursor state, plus rendering tweaks. Well-trodden territory; `tui-input` and `tui-prompts` crates exist if 'wire it ourselves' feels redundant.

title: Future: security profiles with colored/labeled sessions
description:
After Biscuit/capability integration, add configurable security profiles for spawning sessions/agents. Profiles are selected at spawn time from a popup/menu and define the default non-attenuated authority for the new session: readonly, read/write, limited commands, yolo, production-yolo, etc.

UI/UX:
- Each profile has a color/border/badge.
- High-risk profiles such as yolo-in-production render red/red-bordered.
- Readonly uses a calm color.
- Mixed/limited-write uses a medium-warning color.
- Sessions are labelable so operators can distinguish role/authority contexts, similar to tmux panes named machine/root/functional-account.

Security semantics:
- Profile selection mints/attaches the session Biscuit/capability set and tool-policy defaults.
- Child/delegate sessions may attenuate from parent but not widen beyond parent.
- Dangerous profiles should require stronger confirmation.

Surface profile+label in session list/tree, transcript header, approval prompts, and emergency controls.

Related memory: mu_security_profiles_colored_labeled_sessions.

title: Subagent context handoff via shared read-only events + initial pointer-set
description:
Define how a parent session shares context with a spawned subagent without giving the subagent a mutable view of parent state.

## Mechanism

ChildSession { parent_session_id, parent_events_view: ReadOnlyEventLogHandle, initial_pointer_set: Vec<PointerToParentSpan>, own_event_log, own_retained_set (points into BOTH parent and own) }

Subagent receives at spawn: read-only access to parent event log; initial pointer-set; optional synthetic spans summarizing parent context. Writes only to its own log.

## Eviction semantics

Parent compaction drops a pointer to span S: parent's retained set no longer points at S; parent's event log STILL HAS S (append-only); if subagent retained its own pointer to S, the subagent's rope is unaffected. 'Eviction from parent = forgetting that pointer.' Parent compaction never strands subagent.

## Scope

In: ChildSession shape; spawn API; span ID namespace ((SessionId, EventId, SpanWithinEvent) triples); capability attenuation at spawn (subset of parent's allowed predicates); tests; doc.

Out: subagent dispatch UI; cross-subagent communication (mu-008/d22f391a — separate concern); multi-level subagent trees (start depth-1).

## Why

Today's options: copy parent context (expensive, stale) or give nothing (rebuild from brief — wasteful). Shared-read-only-events + initial-pointers: subagent has cheap parent access (read-only handle, not copy); independent retained set; provenance through both event logs.

## Acceptance

Child spawned with initial pointer-set has those pointers in retained rope; child's rendered AgentView includes parent-span content; parent compaction dropping span S does NOT affect child's retained pointer to S; child's event log distinct; doc covers shape + eviction + capability attenuation; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-5-subagent-context-handoff.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-nat
Memories: d22f391a, 5b69bcdf (cooperating-sessions mailbox — related, distinct)
Existing: event-sourced-context.md Session tree section

title: BUG: orphan mu serve / silent Popen hang under background-bash spawning
description:
Memory a1dd04fa has the full diagnostic pattern.

Symptom: Python orchestrator using MuClient hangs forever on fresh ask. No mu serve child appears in process tree; python sits in I (idle) syscall-state with ~0 CPU. No stderr. No exception.

Reproduces under Bash run_in_background=true especially with stale mu serve processes around. Foreground works.

Suspected mechanism: subprocess.Popen fork window stretching indefinitely under background-bash supervision. Possibly compounded by file-lock contention on ~/.config/mu/auth/openai-codex.json across daemons.

Workaround SHIPPED: mu_client.py defensive layer (jj qmlywmnm 186d5970, claude-personal scripts). read_timeout + on_progress + stderr drainer + orphan warning. Failures now surface as MuClientTimeout instead of silent stalls.

Real fix unclear without more diagnosis. Lower priority because workaround makes the failure visible rather than mysterious — but worth chasing when bandwidth allows.

title: TUI: implement F5 usage / cache dashboard
description:
Per mockup, F5 shows cumulative usage by provider/model, cost estimate, cache hit %, and a cache ledger panel.

Cumulative usage already exposed via daemon.stats (total_input_tokens, total_output_tokens). Per-provider breakdown needs a session.list grouped/aggregated client-side OR a new daemon.usage_by_provider RPC. Start with client-side aggregation; promote to RPC if needed.

Cost estimation needs a per-provider price table — track as a separate issue.

title: BUG: cargo test --workspace fails intermittently — env-var mutation in tool tests races with parallel test execution
description:
## Symptom

`cargo test --workspace` fails non-deterministically; observed today (2026-05-11 ~19:00 local) with:

- tools::grep::tests::b4_count_mode
- tools::grep::tests::b6_case_insensitive

Same assertion: `assert!(!result.is_error)`. `cargo test -p mu-coding tools::grep` alone passes (11/11). Running b4 in isolation also passes. Task 19603420 reported all 278 workspace tests passing at 14:59 — the race window has just widened recently (more total tests = higher concurrency pressure).

## Root cause

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs::tests::rg_unavailable_returns_clean_error` (line 534) mutates the process-global `MU_RG_BINARY` env var:

```rust
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", "/no/such/binary/rg");
// ... test runs ...
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", original);  // or remove_var
```

Parallel `#[tokio::test]`s in the same test binary call `locate_rg()` which reads `MU_RG_BINARY`. Any test reading the var during the bogus-value window fails. b4 / b6 lose this race more often than b5; bash tests don't currently fail because their env keys (`MU_TEST_BASH_*`) don't interact with the rg path, but the same anti-pattern exists.

## Same anti-pattern elsewhere

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs` has two tests that mutate process env:

- tests::b5_strict_env_scrub (line 541): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_API_KEY`
- tests::b10_yolo_env_passes_through (line 598): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_YOLO_VAR`

These currently don't race against anything (no concurrent reader of those keys) but the pattern is fragile.

## Rust 1.74+ context

`std::env::set_var` is documented as not thread-safe and is `unsafe` from Rust 2024 edition onward. `cargo test` runs tests in threads by default. So any `set_var` in a test is a future-trap.

## Fix options

A. **Minimal (chosen for triage)**: `#[ignore]` the actual offender; document follow-up here. Loses one test path's coverage; that path ("rg not found") is structurally trivial. Done in commit landing alongside this bead.

B. **Durable**: refactor `locate_rg` so the rg binary path can be injected at `GrepTool` construction time. Tests pass a bogus path directly, no env mutation needed. Same shape applies to bash.rs (inject env scrub policy / passthrough policy as struct config rather than process env). Estimate: ~1 hour each.

C. **Standard fix**: add `serial_test` to workspace dev-deps; `#[serial]` the three offenders. Quick, adds one dep. Doesn't fix the root cause (env mutation in tests) — just serializes it.

## Suggested

Land A now (in this conversation's commit). Open follow-up beads referencing this one to do B for grep and B for bash separately.
